title: Blackouts Are Prabowo’s Latest Problem
gist: Foreign Policy's Southeast Asia Brief reports that Indonesia's June 2026 rolling blackouts stem not from a supply shock but from the Prabowo government's own decision to cut coal production quotas to boost export prices, backfiring into a domestic shortage with estimated losses of $173-562 million — one of several regional flashpoints covered, alongside Bangkok's landslide mayoral reelection, the politically charged conviction of Gojek founder Nadiem Makarim, Hanoi demolition protests, and a Singapore language debate sparked by a hit Teochew-language film.

insights.claim: Indonesia's June 2026 rolling blackouts across Java, Sumatra, Bali, and Indonesian Borneo were caused by a coal shortage the government created itself, not by a global energy shock, after officials cut coal production quotas earlier in the year specifically to push up global prices and boost export earnings.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A policy-induced, not structural, supply failure means the fix is a policy reversal rather than new capacity investment — relevant for anyone pricing Indonesian coal-export volumes or power-sector risk.
insights.locator: Instead, the government has admitted after some early denials

insights.claim: Faced with a gap between low mandated domestic coal prices and high international prices, Indonesian miners sold as much coal as possible abroad even where this meant missing their 'domestic market obligation,' or exported high-quality coal while dumping low-quality, less energy-dense coal onto the domestic market.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Shows Indonesian coal miners will arbitrage domestic price controls at the expense of compliance whenever the export-domestic price gap widens enough — a repeatable dynamic to watch whenever Jakarta manipulates quotas again.
insights.locator: Faced with the gap between low domestic prices

insights.claim: An industry insider directly disputed the Indonesian government's claim that the blackout problem has been resolved, calling it a 'complete lie' and warning that worse may be to come.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: An on-the-ground source contradicting the official line suggests the blackout risk is understated in government messaging, warranting caution on any Indonesia power-sector or industrial-continuity assumptions through summer 2026.
insights.locator: an industry insider countered that this is a “complete lie”

insights.claim: Initial estimates put the economic losses from the June 2026 blackouts at between $173 million and $562 million, and business leaders warn that fear of future blackouts is becoming an additional deterrent to investment in Indonesia.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A quantified, sizable near-term GDP/investment drag directly attributable to an avoidable policy choice — a concrete input for Indonesia macro and FDI-flow models.
insights.locator: Initial estimates of the losses already caused

insights.claim: Java had more electricity generation capacity than it needed for years due to 2010s overbuilding, confirming the June 2026 shortage is a policy-induced supply failure rather than a structural capacity gap; a separate three-day blackout that hit Sumatra in May 2026 was caused by weather-related transmission-line disruption, not coal supply.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Distinguishing the two blackout causes (policy-driven coal shortage vs. weather-driven transmission failure) clarifies that Indonesia's grid has multiple independent failure modes, raising the base rate of future outage risk beyond just the coal-quota issue.
insights.locator: For years, Indonesia actually had more electricity generation capacity

insights.claim: The blackouts compound a growing perception of dysfunction under President Prabowo Subianto, whose nationalist self-sufficiency economic policies have caused broader economic turmoil, and arrive alongside renewed increases in food and fuel prices and active student protests over economic mismanagement.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A convergence of blackouts, inflation, and street protests is a classic pre-instability pattern for Indonesia, worth flagging for any near-term political-risk assessment on the country.
insights.locator: It is also another blow for President Prabowo Subianto

insights.claim: Indonesia saw its worst riots since 1998 in August 2025 against a backdrop of rising food prices, and the recurrence of both rising prices and blackouts heading into summer 2026 raises the risk of renewed unrest.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Gives a specific historical precedent and trigger pattern (food-price spikes plus visible service failures) that investors can use as an early-warning indicator for Indonesian social unrest.
insights.locator: August of last year saw Indonesia’s worst riots

insights.claim: Bangkok's independent governor Chadchart Sittipunt won reelection with 67.8% of the vote, up from 52.7% in 2022, while his former party Pheu Thai — the Shinawatra family's political vehicle — has been reduced to a junior national coalition partner, fueling speculation Chadchart could seek the premiership despite denying interest.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A rising independent political figure eclipsing the dominant Shinawatra-aligned party is a signal worth tracking for Thai political-risk positioning ahead of any future national election.
insights.locator: Chadchart Sittipunt, the independent governor of Bangkok

insights.claim: Gojek founder and former Education Minister Nadiem Makarim was sentenced to prison on June 30, 2026 over Chromebook procurement corruption charges that critics call thin and politically motivated; Indonesia's loosely defined corruption law leaves considerable scope for such prosecutions, and who pressured prosecutors to pursue a case against a non-outspoken former official remains unexplained.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A high-profile, possibly politically motivated prosecution of Indonesia's most prominent tech founder is a chilling-effect risk for entrepreneurial and foreign investment sentiment toward Indonesia's tech sector.
insights.locator: Indonesian tech company founder and former Education Minister Nadiem Makarim

insights.claim: Hanoi residents are staging rare public protests, including prayer vigils and critical banners, against a multitrillion-dollar government plan to demolish and rebuild the capital that would relocate roughly 250,000 people along the Red River on a promise of $95,000 average incomes by 2065; Vietnamese land disputes have turned deadly before, including a 2020 case near Hanoi that killed three police officers and one villager and led to two death sentences.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A 250,000-person forced-relocation plan with a track record of lethal land disputes is a material political and execution risk for any capital deployed into Vietnam's Hanoi redevelopment program.
insights.locator: Residents of Hanoi are taking part in rare protests

insights.claim: A Teochew-language film, 'Dear You,' became a Singapore box-office hit and reignited debate over the government's decades-long policy of promoting Mandarin over southern Chinese varieties (Teochew, Hokkien, Hakka, Cantonese), forcing officials to add 50 more Teochew screenings on top of the original 22 in response to demand.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Shows measurable consumer demand for non-Mandarin Chinese-language content forcing a policy concession in Singapore, relevant to media-localization strategy in the market.
insights.locator: The government gave a little ground

insights.claim: Singapore's government is wary of loosening its Mandarin-first language policy in part because it fears narratives of Chinese ancestral loyalty could become a vector for PRC influence campaigns, a concern sharpened by a recent wave of China-origin videos stoking fears about the 'Indianization of Singapore.'
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Flags a specific, named influence-operations concern (China-origin videos targeting Singapore's ethnic-Chinese identity) relevant to assessing PRC information-operations risk in Southeast Asia.
insights.locator: It is wary of narratives promoting loyalty

insights.claim: Indonesia's Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs Arif Havas Oegroseno has compared the country's waterways to the Strait of Hormuz, arguing that smaller regional powers can now effectively deny great-power naval access there.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: Signals Jakarta may be building a strategic-leverage narrative around its own maritime chokepoints, relevant to how Indonesia positions itself in future great-power naval-access negotiations.
insights.locator: Indonesian Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs Arif Havas Oegroseno

insights.claim: Analysts Yuhang Ding and Sailin Li argue in the Diplomat that Russia's invasion of Ukraine has eroded a traditional pillar of Moscow's regional presence — arms sales — cautioning against overestimating Russia's continued influence in Southeast Asia.
insights.speaker: Joseph Rachman
insights.so_what: A weakening Russian arms-export position in Southeast Asia is an opening for Western and other defense exporters to gain regional market share.
